title: Journal of Inquiry-Based Learning in Mathematics
text:
JIBLM publishes freely downloadable, professionally refereed and classroom-tested course guides for full univerisity level inqury-based mathematical courses.
Inquiry-based learning (IBL) refers to any pedagogy that utilizes presentations or student-centered activities to develop in students the confidence and ability to do mathematics on their own. Instructors typically supply students with carefully crafted activities or course notes consisting of a sequence of definitions, problems or theorems. Instructors then serve as mentors, listening to the students, reviewing their work, and giving them the minimal information they need to understand the concepts and carry out these tasks.
Instructors are encouraged to contact authors of Course Guides for assistance. Anyone reproducing or displaying JIBLM publications should clearly display the journal name, title, and issue. Reproduced materials may be distributed provided that no charge is levied to users beyond cost of reproduction.
JIBLM was previously funded by MathNerds and the Educational Advancement Foundation, and is currently funded through private donations.

This code chunk takes a single row from metadata and transposes it so we can display a table with it’s entries. We also take the column names from metadata for the labels of that table. Ultimately, this is the R code that we want to write to multiple .qmd files








title
An Inquiry-Based Introduction to Proofs


subject
foundations


pages
33


issue
No. 47 (Sept. 2018)


author
Hefferon, Jim


institution
St Michael's College


contact
jhefferon@smcvt.edu


abstract
An introduction to mathematical proof. Covers elementary number theory including divisibility and primes, sets including DeMorgan's laws, functions including one-to-one and onto, and relations. There is a brief optional chapter on cardinality. The presentation is a sequence of statements of definitions and results, with a few remarks.


vol
47

title: Precalculus: An Inquiry-Based Approach
text:
Title:
Precalculus: An Inquiry-Based Approach


Subject:
precalculus


Pages:
140


Issue:
No. 56 (March 2023)


Author:
Howard, Paul K. < Mahavier, W. Ted


University:
Oklahoma Christian University and Self-Employed


Contact:
paul.howard@oc.edu, ted.mahavier@gmail.com


Abstract:
A standard precalculus course covering polynomials, rationals, exponentials, logarmithmics, trigonometry, polar coordinates, rate of change, and conic sections. This course may also serve as either College Agebra or Trigonometry simply by choosing the appropriate chapters.



Instructor Version Download
Student Version Download

title: Journal Content
text:
title
An Inquiry-Based Learning Approach to Mathematical Modeling


subject
modeling


pages
62


issue
No. 51 (July 2020)


author
Young, Andrea


institution
Ripon College


contact
younga@ripon.edu


abstract
This is a set of notes for a one semester course in Mathematical Modeling. The topics covered are difference equations, Markov chains, Monte Carlo simulations, and linear programming. The notes emphasize scientific computing and include both problems and projects. These notes are written to be used with Sage, but they could be modified to incorporate another language.








Instructor Version
Student Version

This refereed course guide is supplied to you free of charge. In exchange, we ask only that if you reproduce a course guide, you do not sell it for more than the cost of reproduction. At JIBLM, the author retains the copyright to his or her material and many are willing to share the .tex source of their notes. If you wish to have the source in addition to the .pdf available below, we ask that you contact the author directly. The author’s email address at the time of submission is provided above.

title: Journal Content
text:
title
The Story of Statistics: Inquiry based notes for introductory statistics


subject
statistics


pages
184


issue
No. 58 (Nov. 2024)


author
Lindsey Bell, Nicholas Pritchard, and Douglas Weathers


institution
Coastal Carolina University


contact
lbell2@coastal.edu, npritcha@coastal.edu, wweathers@coastal.edu


abstract
Notes for an introductory statistics course emphasizing the process of statistical thinking. Topics include data collection, graphical and numerical summaries, introduction to probability, random variables, sampling distributions, confidence intervals, hypothesis testing, and simple linear regression. A common investigation is woven throughout the notes to provide continuity and hands on activites are used to introduce new conepts.








Instructor Version
Student Version

This refereed course guide is supplied to you free of charge. In exchange, we ask only that if you reproduce a course guide, you do not sell it for more than the cost of reproduction. If you wish to have the source in addition to the .pdf available below, we ask that you contact the author directly. The author’s email address at the time of submission is provided above.

title: Journal Content
text:
title
A First Course in College Calculus


subject
calculus


pages
18


issue
No. 60 (Apr. 2025)


author
Tulsi Srinivasan


institution
Azim Premji University, Bengaluru


contact
tulsi.srinivasan@apu.edu.in


abstract
These notes are for a first course in college calculus/introductory real analysis. The first part of the course motivates the study of sequences and their convergence through problems involving calculating the area under a graph and the slope of the tangent at a point on a graph. The second part draws on observations from the first about structural differences between the reals and the rationals, and starts with some axioms for the reals. These axioms are then used to study continuity and properties of continuous functions on the reals.








Instructor Version
Student Version

This refereed course guide is supplied to you free of charge. In exchange, we ask only that if you reproduce a course guide, you do not sell it for more than the cost of reproduction. title: Journal Content
text:
title
Point-Set Topology


subject
topology


pages
21


issue
No. 61 (Nov. 2025)


author
M Padraig McLoughlin << Michel Smith << W.S. Mahavier << R.L. Moore


institution
Kutztown University


contact
mcloughl@kutztown.edu


abstract
These notes offer a general introduction to Point-Set Theory. The notes offer the student the opportunity to investigate limit points, closed or open sets, compactness, countability, sequences, continuity, connectedness, sseparability, local compactness, and total connectedness.








Instructor Version
Student Version

title: Journal Content
text:
title
Discrete Math (Introduction to Proof)


subject
discrete math


pages
89


issue
No. 59 (Apr. 2025)


author
Rachel Schwell


institution
Central Connecticut State University


contact
schwellrac@ccsu.edu


abstract
This text covers a collection of standard topics in an introduction-to-proof course: logic, techniques of proof including induction, set theory, functions, and introductory number theory. Sets and functions (including injectivity and surjectivity) are given special attention, in preparation for upper-level courses such as abstract algebra. The text is intended to be standalone in an IBL setting with no supplementary lecture or notes required and is scaffolded accordingly. It is meant to be user-friendly, while still encouraging readers to stretch their thinking and develop mathematical habits of mind.








Instructor Version
Student Version

This refereed course guide is supplied to you free of charge. In exchange, we ask only that if you reproduce a course guide, you do not sell it for more than the cost of reproduction. At JIBLM, the author retains the copyright to his or her material and many are willing to share the .tex source of their notes. If you wish to have the source in addition to the .pdf available below, we ask that you contact the author directly. The author’s email address at the time of submission is provided above.

title: Calculus I – Single Variable Calculus
text:
Title:
Calculus I – Single Variable Calculus


Subject:
calculus


Pages:
90


Issue:
No. 50 (May 2019)


Author:
George, C. Yousuf; Lewis, Heather A.


University:
Nazareth College


Contact:
cgeorge0@naz.edu


Abstract:
This course introduces students to the fundamentals of calculus, its notation and its applications. Some of these concepts include: functions, rates of change, limits, derivatives, applications of derivatives, antiderivatives, integration, and the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus.

title: Finite Mathematics
text:
Title:
Finite Mathematics


Subject:
finite math


Pages:
92


Issue:
No. 57 (April 2023)


Author:
Mindy Capaldi


University:
Valparaiso University


Contact:
mindy.capaldi@valpo.edu


Abstract:
These notes are for a four-credit, 100-level finite mathematics course. Topics include matrix algebra, linear programming, probability, counting methods, Markov chains, and game theory. In practice, studuents work through the notes during class, complete problems in groups, and present their work at the board. Two modules using board games are also included as appendices. The notes can be easily adapted by taking out explanatory passages or examples, based on the mathematical background of the students.



Instructor Version Download
Student Version Download

title: Introduction to Combinatorial Games
text:
Title:
Introduction to Combinatorial Games


Subject:
game theory


Pages:
52


Issue:
No. 52 (Oct. 2020)


Author:
Gantner, Ryan


University:
St. John Fisher College


Contact:
rgantner@sjfc.edu


Abstract:
Through this course, students will build a robust theory of combinatorial games from scratch, including theories of partisan games, impartial games, nimbers versus numbers, the “” function, and basic Sprague-Grundy theory. Several games will be introduced, such as Nim, Hackenbush, Kayles, and Chomp. These notes are aimed at students who are not math majors and who have not taken college math courses previously. With only slight modification, however, this could become a course for math majors.



Instructor Version Download
Student Version Download

Contact:
rdunning1@stmarytx.edu


Abstract:
A mathematics course designed for students in the humanities. A variety of concepts are covered. Topics will be drawn from the following areas: introductory treatments of sets, logic, number theory.

title: Linear Algebra: Notes and Problems
text:
Title:
Linear Algebra: Notes and Problems


Subject:
linear algebra


Pages:
78


Issue:
No. 53 (Jan. 2022)


Author:
Long, Nicholas


University:
Stephen F. Austin State University


Contact:
longne@sfasu.edu


Abstract:
This set of notes and problems covers a junior-level linear algebra course (following an introduction to proof course) with broad chapter topics of 1) efficiently solving systems of linear equations, 2) vector spaces, 3) connecting ideas (dimension, invertibility, and eignvalues), and 4) innner product spaces. The approach of these notes isa blend of computational and sense-making problems, as well as an increasing usage of conjecture and proof throughout the course.



Instructor Version Download
Student Version Download

132


issue
No. 48 (Dec. 2018)


author
Howard, Paul K. < Mahavier, W. Ted


institution
Oklahoma Christian University


contact
paul.howard@oc.edu


abstract
This course is designed to prepare students to begin a college level calculus series. Topics in this course include all aspects of trigonometry as well as conic sections, exponential functions, polynomial functions, rational functions and linear functions with an emphasis on rate of change. Strong emphasis is placed on strengthening understanding of algebraic concepts necessary to be successful in a college level calculus series.

title: Course Materials
text:
These refereed course notes fulfill the guidelines listed under Information for Authors of Course Notes.
Keeping JIBLM free is a top priority. If you use JIBLM materials, we want to know! Please consider emailing our Course Reviews Editor Nathaniel Miller listing which notes you use, your institution, your name, and anything else you wish to share.
This table is sortable. Click on a column heading to sort by that column. Click on a title to access these notes.

title: Information for Authors of Course Notes
text:
Course Notes are freely downloadable, professionally refereed, classroom-tested notes which constitute a full course or sequence of full courses. Course Notes are intended

for instructors to use as notes for their course
for instructors to modify to suit the needs of their students
for students to use to study a topic independently.

Instructors are encouraged to contact authors for guidance in the use of Course Notes.

Publication Requirements

Abstract
Course Notes should include an abstract with sufficient detail to assist users who are viewing multiple sets of Course Notes in determining which set best meets their needs.


Classroom Testing
Course Notes should be thoroughly class tested and provide a successful course in their present formulation. Course Notes that have not been thoroughly vetted through multiple cycles of classroom testing rarely pass the refereeing process.


Content
Course Notes should be mathematically correct and well written, consisting primarily of a sequence of problems to solve or theorems to prove. Course Notes should provide material for a full course or sequence of courses. The student version, excluding supplementary materials and appendices, should span at most seventy pages per semester of material for the course.


Copyrights
Authors submitting materials retain copyright and cede to JIBLM the right to display published materials in perpetuity. Anyone reproducing or displaying JIBLM publications should clearly display the journal name, title, issue and number. Reproduced materials may be distributed provided that no charge is levied to the users beyond the cost of reproduction.


Introduction
Course Notes should maximize the opportunity for a successful implementation by providing potential instructors with a clear understanding of your implementation of the course. The introduction should therefore include

a description of the course you taught,
the approximate class size,
the number of times the course has been taught,
its level and role within the broader curriculum,
your expectations and grading of the students,
the exact portion of the notes covered in your course,
any unusual characteristics of the course or classroom such as technology, multiple white boards, special seating, etc., and
any other information that will be useful to a less experienced instructor.



Pedagogy
Course Notes should adhere to an IBL pedagogy that utilizes presentations or student-centered activities to develop in students the confidence and ability to do mathematics on their own. Instructors typically supply students with carefully crafted activities or course notes consisting of a sequence of definitions, problems or theorems. Instructors then serve as mentors, listening to the students, reviewing their work, and giving them the minimal information they need to understand the defined concepts, solve the problems, or prove the theorems.


Source Availability
As copyrights are retained by the author, we do not provide the source for download. However, based on our anecdotal experience, authors contacted have been very willing to share their expertise, experience and source with instructors wishing to use the author’s notes. Because authors often revise and improve notes, contacting the author directly may well result in a more current, improved version.


Submissions
Course Notes should be sent via email to the Course Notes Editor David Clark along with a completed Submissions Form using the LaTeX templates provided. The form template.tex serves as:

a guide to typesetting your course notes, and
a template to be modified to produce your print ready submission.



To get started

download the Submission Form, submission_cn.tex,
download the LaTeX template, template_cn.tex,
download the JIBLM Formatting Package, jiblm_cn.tex,
place jiblm_cn.tex and template_cn.tex in the same directory,
compile template_cn.tex and read the output, and
place your notes into a copy of the file template.tex.

Do not modify jiblm_cn.tex. If you encounter any difficulties, please contact the Technical Editor Ted Mahavier for assistance.



Additional Information

Joint Authorship
Some Course Notes come with a pedigree, having been modified from another instructor’s notes. JIBLM encourages such adaptations. To handle these instances, JIBLM adheres to the following two conventions regarding authorship.
For cases where an author modifies another’s notes:
If each of X and Y is an author, then the relation X < Y indicates that the Course Notes were originally authored by Y and subsequently modified by X, who takes full responsibility for the current version. While Y is credited with the genesis of the notes, s/he makes no claim to the accuracy of the current version which may or may not reflect her/his original vision.
For cases where an author reproduces another’s notes, for example, posthumously:
If each of X and Y is an author, then the relation X << Y indicates that these notes were originally authored by Y and subsequently prepared for submission to JIBLM by X without significant modification and with appropriate permissions. While Y is credited with the genesis of the notes, X takes full responsibility for the current version.


Student and Instructor Versions
As an author, you may wish to create both a student and an instructor version of your course notes. The instructor version will consist of the student version along with additional advice, comments and guidance to the instructor that might unnecessarily clutter the student version. Upon publication, both versions will be available on-line from the journal. The downloadable template described below allows you to create one LaTeX document that will produce both versions by toggling one commented line in the beginning of the document.


Submission and Page Costs
There are no submission or page costs associated with publishing in JIBLM.


Updating Course Notes
In order to keep the highest quality notes available online, JIBLM encourages the revision of published notes. Should an author desire corrections or modifications, the author may submit a complete list of revisions along with a new submission to replace the original submission. The revised version will be refereed, and will replace the old version if it is accepted. Such submissions will be replaced in December and June to assure that faculty using course notes on-line will not be effected.

title: Information for Authors of Course Notes
text:

for instructors to use as notes for their course

for students to use to study a topic independently.

Instructors are encouraged to contact authors for guidance in the use of Course Notes.

title: Contact Information
text:
Course Notes Editor
David Clark, SUNY at New Paltz, Emeritus


Associate Editors
Chris Hallstrom, University of Portland
Xiao Xiao, Utica University

title: A Note to Students
text:
Thank you for visiting! This site was built because of our love of teaching students like you!
All the material under Course Notes are full semester courses that you may work through to learn a subject on your own. There aren’t answer keys so you need to be excited about just digging in and learning something independently, but the notes are designed to be self-contained and walk you through the subject.
It’s possible that if you get stuck, then the author will provide guidance. However, we are not a tutoring service, so you can’t expect that level of support.
Oh, I almost forgot! Unrefereed notes are also full courses, but they have not been read by an editor and refereed by a third mathematician. Therefore, we can’t assure you that they are free from errors even though some of these are excellent notes. They also cover topics not covered by our published notes. Enjoy!
